John, can you go through the specific steps that someone would need to go through to set their goals? Sure. I mean, very simple. To me, goal setting is a mathematical equation and it starts like this. Set a goal, number one. Number two, put a date on it. Every goal has to have a date. Then you have short-term goals, which could be every day you could have goals, end of day goals, right? You could make them or not make them by the end of the day and then you discard them and set new goals the next day. So those are ultra short-term goals. But you have short-term goals that are like one month goals, you have three month goals, you have intermediate goals that are like maybe a year, and you have long-term goals that are three years. And then past two, three years, you start getting into sort of more of the vision aspect of things, right? But those are the various goals. They have dates on them. Step three. So number one, set a goal. Two, put a date on it. Step three, identify the people, groups, and organizations that you need to align yourself with to achieve your goal. In other words, in any goal out there, typically you're not going to do it in a vacuum. There's people you need to know, organizations you might need to join, employees you need to hire, whatever it might be, identify all those peoples, those groups, those organizations that you need to have access to. Step four, learn what you need to know. Figure out is there stuff I got to learn? And if there is, educate yourself. Find out what you or hire someone that essentially knows what you need to know. So again, let's go through it. Step one, set a goal. Step two, put a date on it. Step three, identify the peoples, groups, and organizations that you need to align yourself with. Step four, learn what you need to know. Set by develop a plan, meaning the best plan available based on the information you currently have. There are no perfect plans. You don't want to be one of these people that develops a plan forever. I'm still on the planning stage. When I'm done planning, then I'll get started. And there is no perfect plan because the plan that you end up developing will probably change four or five times before you actually end up getting what you want. So but you have to have a plan. So you develop the best plan available based on the information that you currently have about the market, about business itself and your experience. Step six, take massive action. Not a little bit of action. Take massive action towards that goal and go out and do what you got to do. Step seven, do what's called have visual acuity. Analyze your results. See what's working. See what's not working. Anytime we take actions towards a goal, we'll find that some of those actions are actually getting us what we want. And in fact, probably more of those actions in the beginning aren't getting us what we want. So you want to actually, you know, analyze your results, see what's working, what's not and change your approach, which is step eight is one step at a time. Change your approach. So we don't want to change everything at once. But if it's not working, you change one thing, see if it's working again. And you keep running that loop, you know, change your approach, see what's working, change your approach. How many times do you change your approach until you get what you want until you see yourself moving in the right direction? Now there is one caveat to that is that, you know, obviously you don't want to be a moron who's bagging his head against the wall, his or her head against the wall forever. So for a certain amount of times of change your approach, we're still not getting what you want in life. You have to say, hmm, maybe there's something wrong with the actual goal. Maybe the goal was flawed. Maybe I'm missing some of the strategies myself. Although it doesn't occur in a vacuum, you know, good after a certain point in time, you know, you don't want to get, and by the way, this is the danger of becoming emotionally invested in your goals. Because if you become so emotionally invested in your goal and you're not achieving it, you'll become irrational. You'll irrationally be chasing after your goal. You'll keep changing your approach and rather than saying, you know, maybe you should abandon the goals. Maybe it's a bad business. Maybe it's a bad idea altogether. So that's why you want to keep rational instead of becoming irrational and getting emotionally attached to the goal. So those are the steps. Let's go through one more time. Step one, set a goal. Step two, put a date on it. Step three, identify the people's groups and organizations you need to alarm yourself with. Step four, learn what you got to learn. Know what you got to know. Step five, develop a plan of attack. Step six, take massive action. Right? Step seven, analyze your results. And lastly, see what's working and what's not. Change your approach. Right. Simple as that. Although it's been proven that 90% of people don't follow through on their goals. So what's the difference here? How do you have such a higher level of success with people following through and achieving their goals using the straight line system? I would say the problem is, for the most part, is that most people who do set goals don't do it that way. They don't actually set goals, put dates on them, figure out who they got to know to achieve the goal, what they got to learn to achieve the goal. They don't develop a great plan. They spend too much time on the plan. They try to develop the perfect plan when there is none. And they don't have that sensory acuity to keep looking at it and changing their approach and essentially cultivating the art of flexibility, learning how to be flexible when you take action. So you see what's working and what's not. And don't say, no, I'm going to keep doing this and banging my head against the wall whether I like it or not, because that's my plan. My plan is a good plan. So that's, I think, a lot of the reason why people don't achieve goals. And on top of that, though, I think even more important, at least equally as important, is because they don't have the vision on top of their goal. So the goal doesn't have the right meaning. They're not using a goal for what it is. In other words, a goal is not so much about the achievement of it. The goal is just essentially, it's almost like a directional mechanism to make sure you're moving in the right direction. It's not so much about the achievement of the goal. Yeah, of course, you'd rather achieve than not achieve. But at the end of the day, your most important thing is that you're moving towards your vision. So I say that for the people who are, you know, the bulk of people who are setting goals and not achieving them, A, you're probably engaged in flawed goal setting, B, and this is a big one, you're probably a bit light on the strategy of the whole thing. In other words, the beauty of the straight line is that it is a technique, it's a technology that allows you to achieve. You know, go out there and set a goal to become a top producer and you don't know how to become a world-class close. Well, good luck being a top. You can want to be a top producer your whole life. If you don't have the strategy to do it, you're going to be in trouble. So the straight line to me in terms of if you're in sales or, you know, really in business in any way, shape, or form, the straight line is that almost that X factor is that really that almost, let's say, the equalization of the people who are just getting started and allows you to achieve your goals more than not achieve your goals. And whether you do or not is really not that important as long as above those goals, you have a vision that keeps you moving in the right direction. So those two combined, I think are really the core, those three, excuse me, they're really the three core reasons that people don't achieve. A, they just engage in flawed goal setting. B, they lack the strategy to get what they want in life. They don't know how to sell and end up becoming closer. They don't have a straight line, you know, tactic to close everyone is closable. And three, they don't have the vision on top of that, keep them inspired. And with that comes their why and everything we've already talked about, which essentially keeps you moving in the direction you need to move in when the barriers and obstacles present themselves as they always do. And instead of you getting negative and feeling disempowered, that why and that vision, it allows you to essentially be irresistible and irresistible force moving towards your goal and breaking down the barriers in your way. That's simple.
